title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Tax Havens: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Offshore Profit Shifting
epigraph: Sovereignty is the product. Secrecy is the service.
executiveSummary:
The global tax haven architecture shelters approximately 492 billion dollars in annual tax revenue from sovereign collection. This system enables 1.42 trillion dollars in corporate profit shifting and conceals 7.6 to 14.2 trillion dollars in personal wealth offshore. Standard analysis incorrectly frames this as merely a revenue problem. It is not. It is a profound system welfare problem, quantified by the System Asset Pricing Model. This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model, developed in Postnieks (2026a), to the offshore finance system. Six welfare-damage channels are defined and calibrated: Direct Fiscal Drain, Public Services Degradation, Inequality Amplification, Financial Stability Erosion, Governance Capture, and Developing Country Compounding. Each channel is estimated using robust data from the Tax Justice Network, the European Union Tax Observatory, Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Country-by-Country Reporting statistics, International Monetary Fund fiscal assessments, and the Financial Stability Board's Non-Bank Financial Intermediation monitoring framework. The headline result is unequivocal: the system welfare beta for the tax haven architecture is βW = 6.27, with a 90 percent confidence interval of 5.1 to 7.8. This means that for every dollar of private payoff generated by the offshore system, 6.27 dollars of global system welfare are destroyed. The system-adjusted payoff, which is the private payoff net of system welfare cost, is a staggering negative 2.59 trillion dollars annually. This welfare deficit exceeds the Gross Domestic Product of the United Kingdom, generated fresh every twelve months. The break-even remediation rate, denoted as μ*, is 0.84. This implies that the global community would need to recover 84 percent of the annual welfare damage merely to restore the system-adjusted payoff to zero. No existing or proposed reform achieves this threshold. The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two global minimum tax, at its maximum theoretical efficacy, recovers approximately 7 to 12 percent of the calibrated welfare damage. This is an order of magnitude below the necessary break-even threshold. Pillar Two, the most ambitious reform in history, closes one-eighteenth of the required gap. The paper proves an impossibility theorem, Theorem 8.1, which states that three foundational axioms of the offshore architecture—Sovereignty Shield, Frictionless Capital Transit, and Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition—cannot simultaneously hold while maintaining non-negative system welfare. The formal proof is presented in Section 8. This means the tax haven system is not merely inefficient or broken; it is structurally incompatible with global welfare optimization under the Private Pareto Theorem framework. The system cannot self-correct through market mechanisms or incremental reforms. It is axiomatically incompatible with human flourishing. Developing countries bear a disproportionately heavy burden. They lose four times more relative fiscal capacity than developed countries to the same profit-shifting architecture. This loss compounds at 36 percent over a decade through a debt-driven substitute channel, where lost tax revenue is replaced by high-interest sovereign borrowing. The governance capture channel, where professional services firms and haven states actively block meaningful reform, is identified as a load-bearing wall of the offshore architecture. This channel ensures that the actors who benefit most from the system are precisely those with the greatest influence over reform processes. The Monte Carlo simulation, involving 100,000 draws from conservative parameter distributions, reveals that the probability of the system being welfare-destructive (βW > 1) is 100 percent. The minimum simulated βW was 3.2, confirming that under no plausible parameterization does the tax haven system generate more welfare than it destroys. The system is unconditionally welfare-destructive. The Payoff-System Frontier is concave, with a concavity parameter κ = 1.41. This indicates that marginal system damage accelerates with extraction, meaning the next dollar of extraction from the offshore system inflicts 8.90 dollars in marginal welfare damage, 44 percent more than the average. Delay in addressing this issue is superlinearly costly. This paper provides the first System Asset Pricing Model calibration for the global tax haven system, offering a unified welfare metric that integrates fiscal, distributive, stability, governance, and development damage channels. It quantifies the severe limitations of current reform efforts and provides formal grounds for structural reform rather than incremental patching. The evidence is laid out. The theorem is proved. The only remaining question is what to do about it.

keyFindings: The tax haven architecture generates a system welfare beta (βW) of 6.27. This means that for every dollar of private payoff captured by actors within the offshore finance system, 6.27 dollars of global system welfare are destroyed across six calibrated channels. The 90 percent confidence interval for this beta is 5.1 to 7.8, derived from 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. This finding is robust, with zero scenarios in the simulation yielding a βW of 1.0 or less, confirming the system is unconditionally welfare-destructive.

The total annual system welfare cost of the tax haven architecture is estimated at 3.08 trillion dollars. This figure exceeds the Gross Domestic Product of the United Kingdom. This cost is distributed across six channels: Direct Fiscal Drain, Public Services Degradation, Inequality Amplification, Financial Stability Erosion, Governance Capture, and Developing Country Compounding. The magnitude of this destruction underscores the systemic nature of the problem, extending far beyond simple revenue loss.

The system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) for the tax haven architecture is negative 2.59 trillion dollars annually. This metric, calculated as the private payoff (492 billion dollars) minus the total system welfare cost (3.08 trillion dollars), indicates that the system destroys 2.59 trillion dollars more welfare than it delivers to its private beneficiaries each year. This substantial negative value demonstrates the profound net welfare destruction caused by the offshore system.

The break-even remediation rate (μ*) is 0.84. This means that the global community must eliminate 84 percent of the system's annual welfare damage merely to restore the system-adjusted payoff to zero. This threshold is critical for policy evaluation, as it defines the minimum effectiveness required for any reform to prevent further net welfare destruction. Current reforms fall drastically short of this requirement.

The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two global minimum tax, at its maximum theoretical efficacy, recovers at most 12 percent of the calibrated system welfare damage. After accounting for substance-based carve-outs, the Bermuda Qualified Refundable Tax Credit gaming mechanism, and the United States Side-by-Side safe harbor, the effective recovery drops to approximately 4.5 to 5.3 percent. This is less than one-seventh of the break-even remediation rate, highlighting the radical insufficiency of the most ambitious international tax reform to date.

The paper proves an impossibility theorem (Theorem 8.1) demonstrating that three foundational axioms—Sovereignty Shield, Frictionless Capital Transit, and Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition—jointly preclude non-negative system welfare. This structural incompatibility means that no institutional arrangement satisfying all three axioms can yield a positive system-adjusted payoff. The offshore system's welfare destruction is not merely a policy failure but an axiomatic consequence of its design.

Developing countries suffer disproportionately, losing four times more relative fiscal capacity than developed countries to the same profit-shifting architecture. This loss is compounded at 36 percent over a decade through a debt-driven substitute channel, where lost tax revenue forces high-interest sovereign borrowing. The channel-specific beta for developing country compounding is βW,6 = 12.4, the highest among all channels, underscoring the severe and regressive impact on the Global South.

The Payoff-System Frontier for the tax haven architecture is concave, with a concavity parameter (κ) of 1.41. This indicates that marginal system damage accelerates with extraction. The marginal system beta at the current operating point is 8.9, meaning the next dollar of extraction from the offshore system inflicts 8.90 dollars in welfare damage, 44 percent more than the average. This concavity implies that delaying structural reforms will lead to superlinearly increasing costs.

The governance capture channel (Channel 5) is a load-bearing wall of the offshore architecture, contributing 344 billion dollars in attribution-adjusted welfare cost. This channel, characterized by regulatory capture by professional services firms and haven states, actively prevents effective remediation across all other channels. The PricewaterhouseCoopers Australia scandal serves as a concrete example of this strategic duplicity, where firms advise governments on anti-avoidance laws while simultaneously marketing circumvention strategies.

All five crossover thresholds, representing points where cumulative system welfare damage permanently exceeded cumulative private payoff, have already been breached. The last one, the Reform Futility Crossover, occurred around 2024, coinciding with the compromised implementation of Pillar Two. This means the tax haven system is not approaching a point of no return; it passed the last one before most readers opened this paper, indicating that the system has been net welfare-destructive for decades.

The tax haven system ranks among the most destructive private-gain systems globally, with βW = 6.27. This places it ahead of Bitcoin mining (5.0), antimicrobial resistance (2.1), and nuclear power (0.7), though behind more extreme domains like PFAS contamination (35.2) or the Electric Reliability Council of Texas grid design (2,053). The absolute welfare footprint of 3.08 trillion dollars annually is one of the largest across all System Asset Pricing Model domains, highlighting its immense societal cost.

theorem.formal:
Theorem 8.1 (Jurisdictional Tax Haven Impossibility), also known as Theorem 16 in the Postnieks Program sequence, formally states the structural incompatibility of the global tax haven system with non-negative system welfare. Let J = {1, ..., J} be a set of sovereign jurisdictions with J ≥ 3, including at least one "small" jurisdiction (with domestic tax base B_j < {B}) and at least one "large" jurisdiction (with B_j ≥ {B}). Under three specific axioms – Axiom 1: Sovereignty Shield (SS), Axiom 2: Frictionless Capital Transit (FCT), and Axiom 3: Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition (JRC) – the following conditions hold:

(i) The Nash equilibrium tax rate vector {t}* = (t_1*, ..., t_J*) satisfies t* < t^G, where t^G is the globally welfare-maximizing uniform rate. This means that the tax rates resulting from individual jurisdictional competition are lower than what would be optimal for global welfare.

(ii) At this Nash equilibrium tax rate vector {t}*, the total system welfare cost W({t}*) exceeds the total private payoff Π({t}*), yielding a system welfare beta βW({t}*) > 1. This signifies that the system, at its competitive equilibrium, destroys more welfare than it generates in private benefit.

(iii) Consequently, the System Welfare Sharpe Ratio S_W({t}*) < 0. This is the ultimate conclusion: no institutional arrangement that simultaneously satisfies all three axioms can yield non-negative system welfare. The system is not merely inefficient; it is axiomatically incompatible with global welfare optimization.

This theorem is classified as a Class 3–Class 3 Hybrid Impossibility, indicating a dual institutional impossibility operating at both the international system level and the domestic governance level. The theorem is robust and holds for any configuration of jurisdictions meeting the specified conditions, provided the axioms remain empirically valid. The impossibility is structural, not political, and implies that market mechanisms alone cannot resolve the welfare deficit.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a game where every player tries to win by making their own rules, and they can easily move their pieces to whatever part of the board has the most favorable rules. That's essentially the global tax haven system, and this paper proves that such a game can never end well for the overall system.

Think of it like this: there are three fundamental 'rules' or 'axioms' that define how the tax haven system works. First, the Sovereignty Shield axiom means that every country gets to decide its own tax laws, and no one can force them to change. It's like each player in our game having their own personal rulebook that no one else can override. Second, the Frictionless Capital Transit axiom means money can zip across borders almost instantly and without cost. If one country raises its taxes, companies can just move their 'paper profits' to another country with lower taxes. This is like players being able to teleport their pieces to any square they want, making it impossible to trap them. Third, the Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition axiom means every country is trying to attract as much business as possible by offering lower tax rates than their neighbors. It's a race to the bottom, where each player tries to undercut the others to get more pieces on their side of the board.

The theorem says that if these three rules are all in play at the same time, the game is rigged to fail the system. The tax rates that countries end up with (the 'Nash equilibrium') will always be too low to support global welfare. Why? Because each country is only looking out for itself, not the whole world. And because money can move so easily, countries are forced to keep their rates low to avoid losing business. The result is that the total damage to the system (like underfunded schools, rising inequality, and financial instability) will always be much, much bigger than the private gains made by the companies and individuals using tax havens. The System Welfare Sharpe Ratio, which tells us if the system is creating or destroying value, will always be negative. It's an impossibility: you cannot have these three rules simultaneously and expect the system to be healthy. The game itself is designed to destroy value, no matter how cleverly the players play.

axioms.name: Sovereignty Shield
axioms.statement: Each sovereign jurisdiction j ∈ J retains exclusive, non-delegable authority over its domestic corporate tax rate t_j ∈ [0, {t}] and may not be compelled by any supranational body to alter t_j without its explicit consent.
axioms.evidence: This axiom is evidenced by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework operating through voluntary domestic enactment rather than binding supranational mandate. The United States' ability to extract a 'Side-by-Side' safe harbor, exempting its multinationals, and the historical ability of haven states like Ireland and Luxembourg to block European Union transparency directives through Council votes, further confirm this axiom. A binding, non-voluntary global tax authority would violate this axiom, but no such authority currently exists.

axioms.name: Frictionless Capital Transit
axioms.statement: Financial capital K and accounting profits Π_book can be transferred across jurisdictional boundaries at near-zero marginal transaction cost, such that the semi-elasticity of reported profit with respect to the tax rate differential is bounded away from zero: Π,Δ t > 0.
axioms.evidence: Empirical evidence from Tørsløv, Wier, and Zucman (2022) estimates that the semi-elasticity of shifted profits is approximately three times greater than the semi-elasticity of real investment, indicating that paper profits are radically more mobile than physical assets. While the Common Reporting Standard has reduced this semi-elasticity for individual wealth concealment, corporate profit-shifting elasticity remains high, especially with the expansion of digital services. Capital controls, mandatory beneficial ownership registries, and real-time cross-border transaction monitoring would be required to violate this axiom by reducing the semi-elasticity to near zero.

axioms.name: Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition
axioms.statement: Each jurisdiction j sets t_j to maximize its own national welfare function V_j(t_j; {t}_-j, K_j(t_j, {t}_-j)), where K_j is the capital stock attracted to j, taking other jurisdictions' rates {t}_-j as given (Nash behavior).
axioms.evidence: This axiom describes the non-cooperative game of tax competition. Jurisdictions, particularly small economies like Ireland, Luxembourg, and the Cayman Islands, optimize their own welfare by choosing low tax rates to attract foreign capital, as formalized by Kanbur and Keen (1993). This behavior is observed in the low or zero tax rates offered by many haven jurisdictions. Cooperative rate-setting, where jurisdictions maximize global welfare, would violate this axiom, but such coordination is prevented by Axiom 1 and sustained by Axiom 2.

theorem.proofSketch:
The proof of the Jurisdictional Tax Haven Impossibility Theorem unfolds in three interconnected parts, demonstrating how the three axioms—Sovereignty Shield, Frictionless Capital Transit, and Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition—jointly lead to a system that is axiomatically incompatible with non-negative system welfare.

Part (i) establishes that the Nash equilibrium tax rate vector, resulting from individual jurisdictional competition, will always be suboptimal relative to a globally welfare-maximizing rate. Under Axiom 3, Jurisdictional Rate-Setting Competition, each jurisdiction 'j' independently chooses its corporate tax rate 't_j' to maximize its own national welfare function, V_j. This function considers the domestic tax base, 'B_j', which is influenced by its own rate and the rates of competing jurisdictions, and other non-tax welfare components like employment. Axiom 2, Frictionless Capital Transit, dictates that capital is highly mobile, meaning that raising a domestic tax rate will cause capital to flee, negatively impacting 'B_j'. For small jurisdictions, where the domestic tax base is inherently small, the optimal strategy to maximize V_j is to set a very low 't_j' to attract mobile foreign capital. This leads to a 'race to the bottom' in tax rates. Computable General Equilibrium models, such as CORTAX, confirm that the globally welfare-maximizing uniform rate (t^G) is between 16 and 18 percent. However, the Nash equilibrium rates (t*) resulting from this competition, even under the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development Pillar Two framework, are 15 percent or lower after accounting for carve-outs and Qualified Refundable Tax Credits. Axiom 1, Sovereignty Shield, then ensures that no supranational authority can compel jurisdictions to adopt the globally optimal rate if their individual welfare maximization leads to a lower rate. Thus, the system is locked into suboptimal tax rates.

Part (ii) then demonstrates that at this suboptimal Nash equilibrium, the total system welfare cost (W) will inevitably exceed the total private payoff (Π), leading to a system welfare beta (βW) greater than 1. The private payoff, Π(t*), is defined as the tax revenue sheltered by exploiting the gap between the globally optimal rate and these Nash equilibrium rates. This is empirically calibrated at 492 billion dollars annually. The system welfare cost, W(t*), is generated through the six channels calibrated in Section 4. The structural result is that several channels amplify the direct fiscal drain multiplicatively. For example, Public Services Degradation (Channel 2) is amplified by fiscal multipliers, meaning each dollar of lost revenue translates to more than a dollar of welfare damage. Developing Country Compounding (Channel 6) introduces exponential growth in welfare damage due to the debt-driven substitute mechanism, where lost tax revenue is replaced by high-interest borrowing. Governance Capture (Channel 5) adds welfare costs by actively blocking remediation efforts, ensuring that the suboptimal rates persist. The aggregate welfare cost, W(t*), is calibrated at 3.085 trillion dollars. Therefore, βW(t*) = 3.085 trillion dollars / 492 billion dollars = 6.27, which is unequivocally greater than 1. This proves that the system, as it operates under these axioms, is net welfare-destructive.

Finally, Part (iii) concludes that the System Welfare Sharpe Ratio (S_W) will be negative. The System Welfare Sharpe Ratio is defined as the system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) divided by the standard deviation of the system welfare cost (σW). Since ΠSA = Π - W, and Part (ii) established that W > Π, it follows that ΠSA is negative (specifically, -2.593 trillion dollars). With a standard deviation of welfare cost (σW) of 1.370 trillion dollars (from Monte Carlo simulation), S_W = -2.593 trillion dollars / 1.370 trillion dollars = -1.89, which is less than 0. A negative System Welfare Sharpe Ratio indicates that the system destroys welfare faster than the uncertainty in its damage estimate, confirming its fundamental incompatibility with non-negative system welfare. This conclusion holds for all institutional arrangements that simultaneously satisfy the three axioms, as the proofs for (i) and (ii) are general to such conditions. Breaking any single axiom is theoretically sufficient to resolve this impossibility, but each break carries distinct political and economic requirements.

welfareAnalysis.pi: 492 billion dollars. This represents the gross annual private payoff to haven actors, derived from 347.6 billion dollars in corporate tax revenue sheltered from sovereign collection through profit shifting and 144.8 billion dollars from individual offshore wealth concealment. This figure is the direct transfer from public treasuries to private beneficiaries.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: 3.08 trillion dollars. This represents the total annual system welfare cost inflicted on the global system by the tax haven architecture, measured across six distinct channels. This cost is a comprehensive measure of the damage, including direct fiscal losses, public services degradation, inequality amplification, financial instability, governance capture, and disproportionate impacts on developing countries.

channels.name: Direct Fiscal Drain
channels.description: The Direct Fiscal Drain channel quantifies the direct tax revenue that is not collected by sovereign treasuries due to the offshore finance system. Multinational enterprises shift 1.42 trillion dollars in profits to tax havens annually, and individuals conceal 7.6 to 14.2 trillion dollars in offshore wealth. The private gain, which is the tax not paid, totals 347.6 billion dollars in corporate taxes and 144.8 billion dollars in individual taxes, summing to 492 billion dollars per year. This 492 billion dollars represents a dollar-for-dollar transfer from public treasuries to private actors. Unlike most externalities, where welfare cost involves a deadweight loss beyond the transfer, this direct fiscal drain is itself the welfare cost, as every dollar not collected is a dollar unavailable for public investment, redistribution, or debt reduction. The attribution fraction for this channel is 1.0, meaning it is fully attributable to the offshore system. The channel-specific beta (βW,1) is 1.0, establishing the baseline for welfare destruction.

channels.name: Public Services Degradation
channels.description: The Public Services Degradation channel measures the welfare damage resulting from the 492 billion dollars in unfunded revenue, which directly translates into degraded public services such as healthcare, education, infrastructure, and social safety nets. The International Monetary Fund estimates that emerging market economies need an additional 4 percent of Gross Domestic Product annually to achieve key Sustainable Development Goals, with low-income developing countries needing 15 percent. The welfare cost of unfunded services exceeds the revenue lost due to multiplier effects; for example, a dollar of public health spending in a developing country generates more than a dollar of welfare. The weighted global average fiscal multiplier is approximately 1.3. Thus, the total welfare damage from service degradation is 492 billion dollars multiplied by 1.3, equaling 640 billion dollars. With an attribution fraction of 0.85 (acknowledging that not all service degradation is attributable to offshore tax loss), the channel welfare cost is 544 billion dollars. The channel-specific beta (βW,2) is 1.11.

channels.name: Inequality Amplification
channels.description: The Inequality Amplification channel quantifies the welfare cost arising from the offshore system's disproportionate benefits to the ultra-wealthy. The top 0.01 percent of the wealth distribution holds 50 percent of all offshore wealth and pays significantly lower effective tax rates. This system amplifies inequality through a regressive burden shift, where taxes on labor, consumption, or middle-class income replace lost revenue from the wealthy; wealth concentration feedback, as untaxed offshore wealth compounds tax-free; and political capture feedback, where concentrated wealth translates into political influence that further reduces top-end taxation. The welfare cost of this inequality amplification is estimated at 380 billion dollars annually, using the Atkinson inequality index with an inequality aversion parameter of 1.5. With an attribution fraction of 0.70 (as inequality has multiple drivers), the channel welfare cost is 266 billion dollars. The channel-specific beta (βW,3) is 0.54.

channels.name: Financial Stability Erosion
channels.description: The Financial Stability Erosion channel measures the systemic risk generated by the offshore financial system. Offshore jurisdictions attract shadow banking assets by offering zero-tax regimes and light-touch regulation. This creates fragility through 76.3 trillion dollars in high-risk credit intermediation occurring without central bank backstops or deposit insurance. Money laundering, estimated at 800 billion to 2 trillion dollars annually, further contributes to this erosion. The 2008 global financial crisis demonstrated crisis amplification, with 400 billion dollars in Cayman-domiciled Structured Investment Vehicles collapsing. The welfare cost is measured through expected loss, attributing a probability-weighted cost of financial crises to offshore opacity. Using the International Monetary Fund's estimate of 22 trillion dollars in global household wealth destroyed by the 2008 crisis, and an attribution fraction of 0.75, the annualized expected welfare cost of crisis amplification is approximately 577 billion dollars. Including money laundering's macroeconomic drag, the aggregate channel estimate is 590 billion dollars. With an attribution fraction of 0.65, the channel welfare cost is 384 billion dollars. The channel-specific beta (βW,4) is 0.78.

channels.name: Governance Capture
channels.description: The Governance Capture channel quantifies the welfare cost arising from the influence of professional services firms and haven states on regulatory processes. The Big Four accounting firms generate tens of billions in annual tax advisory revenue, creating strong incentives to preserve the offshore system. This capture operates through regulatory capture by these firms, who advise governments on anti-avoidance legislation while simultaneously selling avoidance products (e.g., the PricewaterhouseCoopers Australia scandal); lobbying by haven states within international organizations; the 'Hurtful Eight' Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development nations blocking the United Nations Tax Convention; and the defunding of tax enforcement agencies. The welfare cost is estimated as the welfare gain that would have been realized had governance capture not prevented superior institutional designs. This is estimated at 430 billion dollars annually. With an attribution fraction of 0.80, the channel welfare cost is 344 billion dollars. The channel-specific beta (βW,5) is 0.70.

channels.name: Developing Country Compounding
channels.description: The Developing Country Compounding channel measures the disproportionate welfare damage inflicted on developing nations. These countries face a triple vulnerability: relative fiscal devastation, losing 0.33 to 0.49 percent of Gross Domestic Product to profit shifting versus 0.17 percent for high-income countries; the debt-driven substitute, where lost tax revenue forces borrowing at 6 to 15 percent interest, compounding losses by 36 percent over a decade; and capital flight, exemplified by Sub-Saharan Africa losing 2 trillion dollars to capital flight between 1970 and 2018. The accumulated offshore wealth of 2.4 trillion dollars from Africa exceeds its total public external debt of 720 billion dollars, creating a 'net creditor' paradox. The dollar estimate for this channel is 520 billion dollars, comprising direct tax revenue loss, debt-compounding costs, and capital flight welfare costs. With an attribution fraction of 0.90, the channel welfare cost is 468 billion dollars. The channel-specific beta (βW,6) is 0.95, but relative to the private payoff extracted from developing countries specifically, this beta is 12.4, highlighting the extreme leverage of welfare destruction.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation is a cornerstone of the System Asset Pricing Model methodology, providing robust estimates of the system welfare beta and its confidence interval by accounting for parameter uncertainty across all six welfare-damage channels. The simulation involves 100,000 independent draws, using a fixed seed of 42 for reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers. This ensures that cross-paper comparisons are not affected by simulation randomness. Each draw generates a unique set of channel costs, which are then aggregated to compute a total system welfare cost and, subsequently, a system welfare beta. The methodology for parameter inputs and distributions is meticulously defined. For the private payoff (Π) and the Direct Fiscal Drain (δ1), a log-normal distribution is used with a mean of ln(492) and a standard deviation of 0.15, reflecting the empirical characteristics of tax revenue loss. For the other five welfare-damage channels (Public Services Degradation, Inequality Amplification, Financial Stability Erosion, Governance Capture, and Developing Country Compounding), log-normal distributions are also employed, with means corresponding to their point estimates (640 billion dollars, 380 billion dollars, 590 billion dollars, 430 billion dollars, and 520 billion dollars, respectively) and standard deviations ranging from 0.20 to 0.35, reflecting varying levels of uncertainty. The attribution fractions (λk) for each channel are drawn from truncated normal distributions, with means as specified in Section 4, a standard deviation of 0.10, and bounds between 0.3 and 1.0. A uniform distribution between 1.15 and 1.30 is used for the cross-channel feedback multiplier, capturing the amplification effects between channels. Crucially, the simulation imposes a uniform positive correlation of ρ = 0.3 across all channel pairs. This reflects the empirical observation that welfare channels are not independent; for instance, governance failures often exacerbate other forms of damage. This correlation structure is implemented using a Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix, transforming uncorrelated standard normal draws into correlated ones. Sensitivity analysis reveals that while the median βW is relatively insensitive to the correlation assumption (varying by ±3 percent between ρ = 0.0 and ρ = 0.5), the width of the confidence interval changes substantially. Higher correlation produces wider intervals because extreme draws are more likely when channels move together. After 100,000 draws, the simulation yields a median βW of 6.27 and a mean βW of 6.32, with a 90 percent confidence interval of 5.06 to 7.79. The 5th percentile is 5.06, and the 95th percentile is 7.79. A critical finding is that the probability of βW being less than 1.0 is 0.0000 percent across all 100,000 draws. This means that under no plausible parameterization, within the specified distributions and ranges, does the tax haven system generate more welfare than it destroys. The minimum simulated βW was 3.2, unequivocally confirming the system's unconditional welfare-destructive nature. The coefficient of variation is 0.13, indicating low relative uncertainty for a domain of this size, which translates to unusually high confidence in the estimated βW. The robustness of these results is further confirmed by testing different distribution types (normal, lognormal, and triangular), all of which yield consistent βW estimates.
